Solid-state NMR spectroscopy has emerged as a versatile technique for studying both the local structure and ion mobility of battery materials. We focus on the study of the electrode–electrolyte interphases that form as a product of battery cycling, and artificial coatings that are used in batteries to improve their performance and longevity – both of which can have a crucial impact on battery performance. We also explore the experimental considerations that need to be taken into account, and how advances in NMR methodology have allowed thin coatings and interfaces to be studied.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of a lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery consisting of a graphite anode and LiCoO2 (LCO) cathode. During discharge, Li+ ions migrate through the electrolyte from the anode to the cathode, whilst corresponding electrons pass through the external circuit. Highlighted within this figure are the coatings and interfaces discussed in this review – the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI), cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI), and artificial coatings.

Schematic illustrations depicting DNP polarisation transfer in from (a) an external TEKPol radical (exogenous DNP) and (b) Fe3+ dopants within a TiO2 substrate. (c) 7Li NMR spectra of a model TiO2 substrate with an artificial LixSiyOz coating with (red) and without (grey) DNP enhancement. Figure adapted with permission from Ref. [31]: Haber et al. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 143: 4694–4704. Copyright 2021 American Chemical Society.

(a) INADEQUATE spectrum recorded for an SEI formed on an Si-based anode. (b) and (c) correspond to the regions of interest highlighted in (a) (green and purple respectively). Asterisks denote spinning sidebands. Figure adapted with permission from Ref. [30]: Michan et al. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 138, 7918–7931. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society.

27Al MAS NMR spectrum of 2% Al2O3 coating on LiCoO2 and schematic showing the formation of a LiCo1-xAlxO2 phase as a result of the high annealing temperatures. Figure adapted with permission from Ref. [44]: Han et al. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces, 9, 14,769–14778. Copyright 2017 American Chemical Society.

The (a) 27Al and (b) 7Li MAS NMR spectra for Al2O3 coatings on LiNi0.5Mn1.5O2 (LNMO) and LiAlO2 (LAO). Figure reprinted from Ref. [26]: Mereacre et al. Mater Res Bull, 134: 111095. Copyright 2021, with permission from Elsevier.

(a) 7Li and (b) 29Si MAS NMR spectra, acquired using endogenous DNP with an Fe(III) dopant as the polarising agent. (c) 7Li and (d) 29Si MAS NMR spectra, acquired using exogenous DNP with TEKPol as the polarising agent. 29Si spectra were acquired using the Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill technique (CPMG). (e) Percentage exchange for the uncoated and coated Li and Mn-rich LiNixMnyCoxO2 (LMR-NMC) cathodes (red and green lines respectively) against immersion time. Figure adapted with permission from Ref. [31]: Haber et al. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 143: 4694–4704. Copyright 2021 American Chemical Society.

head.ce:miscellaneous: This review comes from a themed issue on NMR (2022)
Edited by Bradley F. Chmelka and Anne Lesage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the need for energy storage increases, battery technology continues to be developed and improved – aiming for rechargeable batteries that store more energy, charge faster, last longer, and are more sustainable. Solid-state NMR spectroscopy has emerged as a versatile technique for studying both the local structure and ion mobility of battery materials. Here, we explore the use of solid-state NMR to study coatings and interfaces within batteries. We focus on the study of the electrode–electrolyte interphases that form as a product of battery cycling, and artificial coatings that are used in batteries to improve their performance and longevity – both of which can have a crucial impact on battery performance. We also explore the experimental considerations that need to be taken into account, and how advances in NMR methodology have allowed thin coatings and interfaces to be studied.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable batteries are playing an increasingly important role in everyday life and have helped revolutionise the technology sector with the development of portable electronic devices, such as mobile phones and laptops. More recently, it has been recognised that batteries stand to make important contributions in the transition to a greener society by storing intermittent renewable energy within smart grids and by powering electric vehicles [1,2]. A wide range of battery chemistries are now available, each tailored towards specific applications. New battery chemistries also continue to be intensively researched, with the overall aim of increasing energy and power densities, longevity, and sustainability over existing systems. The performance of a battery, and what application it is suited for, is ultimately dependent on the chemistries of the materials it contains [3,4]. Hence, in order to improve and optimise a battery system, it is vital to have a clear understanding of the atomic or molecular structures of the individual chemical components and how these change during operation [5].

For studying the bulk structures of materials, a wide array of analytical tools exists, including X-ray and neutron diffraction, X-ray and optical spectroscopies, electron microscopy, and magnetic resonance. In many cases, these can be applied in situ to monitor structural changes taking place under electrochemical cycling. However, an equally important yet often more challenging aspect of battery chemistry is characterising interfacial structures and processes. Interfacial structures form spontaneously between chemically distinct components during cell assembly and operation – resulting in desirable, as well as undesirable, effects on the cell chemistry. They can also be deliberately introduced to mitigate against degradation or to enhance ion transport and conductivity. Despite their importance, the structural and mechanistic chemistry of interfacial structures is challenging to study because they typically represent only a small proportion of the battery materials and can be highly complex – involving multiple phases/components with differing degrees of local order. They can also be metastable and/or short-lived. In this regard, solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy is well suited to provide detailed chemical information. NMR spectroscopy requires no long-range order, which makes it an excellent tool for studying disordered systems. It is extremely versatile and can be equally applied to liquid, solid, and intermediate phases. Furthermore, it is element selective, so can provide clarity in complex multicomponent systems [6].

In this article, we review applications of solid-state NMR spectroscopy for the study of coatings and interfaces within batteries and battery materials. Whilst there are a number of interfaces, surfaces, and coatings that can be found in a battery, we have chosen to focus this review on NMR studies of the electrode–electrolyte interphases (EEIs) that form as a product of battery cycling and artificial coatings that are introduced to batteries to improve their performance and longevity. We have chosen examples to demonstrate the scope of solid-state NMR spectroscopy as a technique for studying coatings and interfaces. We also explore the experimental considerations that need to be taken into account when studying such coatings and interfaces. To conclude, we reflect on the advancements that have allowed thin coatings and interfaces to be studied and how future developments in NMR methodology may influence our understanding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Background to batteries and relevant interfaces

ce:section.ce:para: The structure of a conventional lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery can be described by the so-called “rocking chair” model illustrated in (Figure 1 ). Here, a cell comprises solid positive and negative electrode materials. These are characterised by an appreciable difference in electrochemical potential and their structures which can reversibly incorporate/intercalate lithium ions. The electrodes are separated by a liquid electrolyte and an electronically insulating porous membrane which prevents direct contact between the electrodes but allows ion transport. During the operation of the cell, a redox reaction occurs split across the two electrodes. Li+ ions migrate out of one electrode, travel through the liquid electrolyte, and intercalate within the other electrode, whilst electrons travel through the external circuit. This either happens spontaneously due to the difference in electrochemical potential (during discharge) or is driven by an external potential applied to the cell (during charge).

In commercial lithium-ion batteries, the cathode material is typically a redox–active transition metal oxide, e.g., LiCoO2 (LCO), and the anode material is graphite. Electrodes are constructed by mixing a solid powder of the active electrode material with a polymeric binding agent and conductive carbon additive to form a slurry which is then cast as a film onto a metal foil current collector. Electrolytes are usually based on a lithium salt, such as LiPF6, dissolved in an organic carbonate-based solvent (a 1:1 mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) being the most common). For research purposes, half cells are often used where electrode materials are cycled against a lithium metal anode. While lithium metal functions well electrochemically as an anode, the formation of dendrites during repeated cycling prevents its use in commercial cells as this can lead to short circuiting and catastrophic cell failure. Hence, another anode, e.g., graphite, is used in full commercial Li-ion cells.

For parameterising battery performance, a number of metrics are used depending on the specific properties desired [4,7,8]. The gravimetric or volumetric capacity is a measure of the charge stored within the battery, whilst the energy density considers the overall energy stored by the battery per mass or volume, also taking into account the voltage difference between the electrodes within each cell. Power density corresponds to the rate at which charge/ions can be inserted in or extracted from the electrodes without a significant decrease in capacity. Another important metric is the cycle life. This is how many times a battery can be charged and discharged before a significant drop in capacity (typically 80% of the initial value). More qualitative metrics are the safety of a cell and the sustainability of the materials used. With the increasing prevalence of batteries in society, these metrics are of growing importance and have led to new battery chemistries such as all-solid-sate batteries for increased safety and sodium-ion batteries for increased sustainability [9,10].

Since the energy density is one of the primary metrics against which batteries are judged, most cells use electrodes with an appreciable difference in redox potential to maximise the cell voltage. Lithium metal and other low voltage anode materials, such as graphite (0.07 – 0.23 V vs Li/Li+) and silicon (0.05 – 0.47 V vs Li/Li+) [8], operate outside the stability window of the electrolyte. As a result, during the first cycle, electrolyte species undergo decomposition at the anode-electrolyte interface, and the breakdown products are deposited on the anode surface forming a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) later. This results in a loss of cell capacity as it removes lithium from the system; however, once formed, the SEI can act as a passivating layer to prevent further degradation. Owing to the thin (several nanometres thick) and disordered nature of the SEI, the structure is not well understood and has been the subject of intense research. The exact composition is dependent on the cell chemistry used but decomposition products including lithium carbonates, oxides, alkoxides, and fluorides are commonly observed. At the cathode, electrolyte decomposition can also occur forming a similar interphase termed the cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI) [11]. However, the mechanism of CEI formation is complex and is thought to arise from transition metal migration and dissolution from the cathode, together with oxygen evolution during cycling. These processes promote electrolyte decomposition at the cathode surface and can also change the cathode structure itself within the interfacial region [12]. The SEI and CEI are important factors in determining the cell function and longevity as they are involved in the the transport of ions between the electrodes and the electrolyte. It is important to understand their chemical structures, as this determines the mobility of lithium ions through it. In addition, an unstable or brittle interphase layer may continue to grow, reducing the cell capacity and ion mobility and increasing the resistance of the cell [11].

In order to have greater control over the interphases formed during cycling, coatings can be employed to mitigate the reactions occurring at the electrode surface. A wide range of materials have been investigated for use as a coating, including inorganic oxide coatings, such as Al2O3, B2O3, and ZrO2 [13,14], and conducting polymers, e.g., poly(3,4-ethyenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) [15]. There are four main functions an artificial coating can perform; however, many coatings play multiple roles at once simultaneously. Firstly, coatings can act as a physical barrier to prevent the electrolyte from coming into contact with the active material in the electrode – in turn preventing unwanted reactions. Secondly, the coating can behave as a HF scavenger, removing the reactive species from the electrolyte arising from the breakdown of PF6 - electrolyte ions. Thirdly, coatings can be used to increase the electrical or ionic conductivity of the electrode. This is particularly important for fast charging/discharging applications. Finally, some coatings are designed to reduce particle cracking as the materials undergo expansion/contraction during cycling [16].

Regardless of the role a coating/interface plays and if it is artificial or formed naturally, it is important to understand its structure and morphology in order to improve and optimise its performance. However, understanding the structure of these layers is not trivial as they are thin and often disordered or amorphous. This means that diffraction methods cannot be used, but many other techniques can provide insight. For example, scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM and TEM) can be used to determine the thicknesses of interphases and coating layers, as well as reveal information on the morphology of the surface [14,17]. Detailed information about the local structure can be obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy [18,19], whilst energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy is used to map elemental composition across a larger sample area [17]. Furthermore, whilst these techniques are typically done ex situ, in situ and in operando methods are actively being developed [20]. In using and developing these approaches, one of the challenges is that fact that the measurement can potentially interfere with the highly sensitive surface chemistry, for example, as a result of electron gun exposure or due to depth profiling [19]. In addition, surface techniques often focus on only a small region of the sample, meaning interesting structural features may be missed. Therefore, it is useful to consider other non-destructive bulk techniques that can offer complementary local structural information, such as solid-state NMR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction to NMR studies for battery research
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state NMR spectra can give a wealth of local structural information arising from the magnetic interactions between the nuclei of interest and its local chemical environment and/or other nuclei in the system. The most obvious interaction is the chemical shift, δ, which changes the resonance frequency of the nucleus, meaning that chemically inequivalent nuclei appear as separate resonances in the NMR spectrum. This is a response to perturbations in the local magnetic field at the nucleus as a result of the surrounding electron density. This is particularly useful, e.g., in the study of 27Al, where species with different coordination environments are readily identifiable by characteristic chemical shift ranges. For crystalline materials, resonances are also separated on the basis of crystallographic inequivalence, meaning that important information can be obtained about the symmetry of the structure.

Another interaction prevalent in solid-state NMR is the dipolar interaction, which is a through-space magnetic interaction between nuclei. This interaction has an inverse-cube distance dependence and so is relatively short-range (typically detectable over a few Ångströms). As a result, it can be used to provide information about short-range proximities in materials. One way in which this can be done is via cross-polarisation (CP) which transfers magnetisation from one nucleus to another via the dipolar interaction. CP can be used to provide signal enhancement by transferring from a high-sensitivity nucleus (e.g., 1H) to a low-sensitivity nucleus (e.g., 29Si), but also provides a means to edit the spectrum on the basis of close spatial proximity of the two spins. This can be a very powerful approach for studying complex systems where it may be desirable to filter the spectrum (e.g., for specific bonding environments).

The quadrupolar interaction is an interaction between the quadrupole moment of the nucleus and the electric field gradient (EFG) at the nuclear position. Since the EFG is related to the symmetry of the local coordination environment, the quadrupolar interaction can be used to obtain information on local bonding geometry and distortions. This interaction affects nuclei with spin quantum number greater than 1/2 so is often encountered in the study of 7Li and 23Na (both spin I = 3/2), as well as many other nuclei present in anode and cathode materials.

In the study of battery materials, and particularly cathode materials containing transition metal ions with unpaired electrons, paramagnetic interactions can also have a significant influence on NMR spectra. This arises via through-space and through-bond interactions between the magnetic moment of the nucleus and unpaired electrons and can shift resonances by hundreds or thousands of ppm. One of the benefits of this interaction is increased chemical specificity as compared to the chemical shift interaction, such that chemically equivalent species that differ in terms of bond lengths or angles of their local environment can have a large shift difference.

Whilst NMR interactions can provide valuable information about the local structure and bonding of the system, they can also significantly complicate the acquisition and interpretation of solid-state NMR spectra. This is a result of the anisotropic nature of these interactions, i.e., they are dependent on the orientation relative to the external field. For powdered solids, this results in a range of shifts or splitting for each crystallite orientation in the sample, leading to broadened line shapes. This broadening can result in a significant decrease in resolution and sensitivity to the point where no information can be obtained. The pronounced magnetic susceptibility of paramagnetic systems can also lead to broadening and shifts that depends upon the particle or electrode shape [21,22].

The most commonly used experimental technique to recover high resolution is magic-angle spinning (MAS), where the sample is spun around an axis inclined at 54.7° relative to the applied magnetic field. This averages some anisotropic interactions to their isotropic values (i.e., those observed in solution). For nuclei with spin I = 1/2, high-resolution spectra can be acquired provided a sufficiently fast MAS rate is used. However, for quadrupolar nuclei (I > 1/2) it is not possible to fully remove the interaction between the nuclear quadrupole moment and the EFG via MAS. Through this interaction, it is possible to obtain useful information about local symmetry; however, MAS NMR spectra often comprise of broadened unresolved line shapes. In this case, two-dimensional multiple-quantum (MQ) MAS NMR experiments may be used to produce high-resolution spectra and separate overlapping resonances.

When studying batteries and battery materials via solid-state NMR, experiments can be carried out ex situ or in situ [6,23,24]. For ex situ experiments, the battery is cycled to a particular state of charge before being dissembled and the material of interest extracted. In situ NMR has the advantage that cells are studied during operation, meaning that intermediate phases formed during cycling can be potentially observed and identified, without the risk of losing metastable or reactive species during cell disassembly. However, since the cell must be physically connected to the external electrochemical apparatus, MAS cannot be used and, therefore, spectra have intrinsically lower resolution. Despite this, the large shift dispersion provided by paramagnetic interactions can enable distinct chemical environments to be identified [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR studies of coatings and interfaces
Experimental considerations for detection and characterisation of surface and interface components
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state NMR has been increasingly used in recent years to study interfaces and coatings in batteries and battery materials. Since interfaces and coatings are very thin and make up only a small proportion of the sample, one of the primary challenges from the point of view of NMR is detecting them with sufficient sensitivity. The most obvious choice is therefore to study high-sensitivity nuclei which give the best chance of observing the relatively small amount of coating/interface in the sample. Common choices therefore include nuclei such as 1H, 7Li, and 19F which have high abundance and high gyromagnetic ratios. However, the high sensitivity of these nuclei can also present a problem in distinguishing and quantifying low-level signals from the interface or coating of interest in the presence of more intense signals associated with the bulk electrode and/or other components in the system.

For the study of CEIs and coatings on cathode materials which contain paramagnetic transition metal ions, the spectral interpretation can be simplified under the assumption that any signals arising from the bulk electrode should be strongly shifted by the paramagnetic interaction. Assuming the concentration of transition metals in the coating/interface is much lower than the bulk, any signals observed within the diamagnetic shift range can be attributed to the species on the electrode surface [25,26]. However, care must be taken to ensure any excess electrolyte or other components such as binder (which also appear within the diamagnetic shift range) are either fully washed away or accounted for in the analysis. For 7Li NMR, this approach still provides limited information in many cases owing to the very small diamagnetic chemical shift range of 7Li (approximately 2 ppm) and often moderate to strong dipolar interactions which broaden the observed resonances [27]. It can be possible to deconvolute overlapping resonances in this diamagnetic region if the expected components and their chemical shifts are known (e.g., LiF, Li2CO3, Li2O, Li2O2); however, obtaining this chemical information for unknown or more complex phases can be challenging.

In this respect, 1H NMR can potentially provide more information owing to its increased chemical shift range (approximately 20 ppm) [28], although most alkylated electrolyte components and breakdown products occur within a smaller range of ∼10 ppm and dipolar broadening can be significant leading to low resolution [29]. For the observation of fluorine-containing species, 19F NMR has the advantage of a significantly larger chemical shift range of up to 800 ppm [28]. This means that it can potentially provide greater chemical specificity for surface species, even in the presence of fluorine/fluoride in the bulk electrode or residual electrolyte. In cases where the shift dispersion is not great enough, 2D correlation experiments, which utilise the dipolar interaction, can be used to identify individual components [30].

Another approach to enable observation of surface groups is to use isotopic enrichment to increase the signal to an observable level. For the study of the SEI/CEI, this can be done by using isotopically enriched electrolyte solvents which are then incorporated into the interphase upon assembly and cycling of the battery. For the low abundance 13C nucleus, enrichment increases the signal intensity of the interphase components to a detectable level and also enables more advanced through-space and through-bond correlation experiments to give increased chemical information. In addition, enriched interphase components can be linked back directly to electrolyte species or specific atoms within electrolyte species to give mechanistic information about layer formation. Isotopic enrichment of the electrode (e.g., using 6Li-enriched metal) can similarly be used to track the movement and exchange of ions between the two electrodes and resulting interphase layers [31].

Another important consideration when studying coatings and interfaces using solid-state NMR is the effect of the substrate on the interphase or coating formed and the influence this has on signal sensitivity. Obviously, in batteries, the substrate is dictated by the choice of battery chemistry under study; however, for SEI studies high surface area analogues of conventional anode materials (e.g., porous and graphene-based carbons, or nanoparticles) can be used to maximise the amount of SEI formed and therefore increase the signal from this component of the cell [32,33]. While these anode materials are not optimal from the point of view of battery performance, the higher amount of SEI to be observed is beneficial from the point of view of NMR sensitivity.

For studying the CEI or cathode coatings, the paramagnetic interaction can be beneficial in distinguishing different environments owing to the increased shift dispersion. However, paramagnetic broadening of the lineshapes also makes spectral interpretation more challenging. For studying the CEI, spectral interpretation is further complicated by the fact that the interphase layer is very thin (at most tens of nanometres). Therefore, in some cases, it can be advantageous to use a diamagnetic analogue, such as Li2RuO3 [34]. Alternatively, paramagnetic ions can be electrochemically converted to diamagnetic ion. For example, when NMC811 is charged to a voltage of 4.6 V, Ni2+ and Ni3+ ions are oxidised to the diamagnetic Ni4+ ion. This sufficiently dilutes the paramagnetism of the cathode material, allowing 13C spectra to be recorded of the organic CEI components [29].

In recent years, dynamic nuclear polarisation (DNP) has been gaining interest as a method for significantly increasing the sensitivity of NMR experiments. DNP uses unpaired electrons in the sample to boost the signal recorded, similar to CP (Figure 2 ). The unpaired electron sources can be in the form of radical containing polarising agents added to the sample (referred to as exogenous DNP), or can be transition metal ions within the sample (endogenous DNP). The enhancements afforded can be very large, up to a theoretical maximum of 660 for 1H, which corresponds to a reduction in experimental time by a factor of 6602 = 435,600 to achieve an equivalent signal-to-noise ratio [35]. This makes it feasible to perform experiments that would be otherwise unfeasible without DNP, such as natural abundance 17O NMR and homonuclear correlations between dilute spins. Additionally, DNP enhances the regions of the sample closest to the polarising agent. This allows for surface sensitivity if exogenous radicals are used or even studies of the interface between a coating and the substrate if a suitable dopant is included in the substrate [31,36]. Therefore, as well as providing signal enhancement, DNP can be used to selectively filter for surface or interfacial regions of the sample, which can be very useful for the study of coatings and interfaces. One consideration when using DNP in this context is the need to avoid chemical interactions between the polarising agent added to the sample and the interphase layer or coating, particularly given the often reactive and unstable nature of the SEI and CEI. For exogenous DNP, care must be taken in the sample preparation step with the choice of a suitable non-aqueous polarising agent and impregnation under inert atmosphere to minimise the chance of degradation of the sample. In this respect, endogenous DNP is advantageous since the polarisation source is within the sample itself and no polarising agent is required. It should be noted however that exogenous DNP typically leads to larger enhancements due to the highly optimised polarising agent structures that enable more efficient polarisation transfer.

Many solid-state NMR studies of interphases formed at the electrode–electrolyte interface have addressed the key question of the overall chemical composition, which remains difficult to determine in many systems. 7Li and 19F NMR studies have enabled the identification of several inorganic species, such as Li2O, LiF, and LixPOFy, based on their chemical shifts [25,30,34]. Moreover, the quantitative nature of NMR means that tracking the intensity of the peaks after different numbers of cycles correlates to interphase growth. For example, Michan et al. observed that the peak at δ = −204 ppm (assigned to LiF) in the 19F spectrum of an SEI on a silicon anode appears after the first cycle, with little growth after further cycles, confirming the stability of the SEI [30]. The quantitative capabilities of solid-state NMR experiments have also been used to determine how much of a given species is present in an interphase layer. This was demonstrated by Castaing et al., who used 7Li and 19F NMR to determine how much LiF was present in the CEI on a LiFePO4 cathode, by comparing integrated intensities of resonances to those of known LiF-electrode mixtures. Interestingly, this method was able to show that whilst LiF was the dominant lithium- and fluorine-containing species, it only accounts for 13% of the mass gained when the CEI forms, supporting the idea that organic components, invisible to 19F NMR, are the main constituent of the CEI [25].

In addition to inorganic components, organic components are thought to play an important role in determining the ionic conductivity and flexibility of the interphase layer [32]. The narrow chemical shift range of 7Li makes unambiguous assignment of lithium-containing organic species challenging when used in isolation. 13C NMR provides much greater chemical specificity but can be more time-consuming owing to the low natural abundance of 13C and the small proportion of interphase in the sample. Huff et al. used 7Li and 13C MAS NMR to monitor the formation and evolution of the SEI on a graphite anode during cell assembly and subsequent cycling [33]. 7Li MAS NMR showed one main resonance for all samples which was attributed to a variety of inorganic species such as Li2O, Li2O2, and Li2CO3. 13C direct polarisation MAS NMR revealed the presence of C–O–X and CO groups which were attributed to breakdown products of the EC-DMC electrolyte used including polymerised polyethylene oxide (PEO) fragments. Interestingly, a quantitative analysis of the spectra showed a reduction in the total amount of SEI species observed after delithiation, suggesting that the SEI undergoes partial redissolution into the electrolyte during this step.

Although 13C NMR spectra can be acquired at natural abundance, the use of isotopically enriched solvents can significantly increase sensitivity and also allow for a wider range of NMR experiments. In particular, the higher concentration of NMR-active spins in the sample allows for two-dimensional (2D) through-space and through-bond correlation experiments to be carried out, as demonstrated by Michan et al. (Figure 3 ) [30]. In this work, 2D INADEQUATE homonuclear correlation experiments were performed on silicon anodes cycled in a 13C-isotopically enriched EC-DMC electrolyte. The observed correlations enabled detailed assignments of components present, even differentiating between –OCH2CH2O– fragments belonging to PEO and ROCO2Li, which appear at very similar chemical shifts.

However, whilst signal intensity and chemical specificity can be increased using isotopic enrichment, more complex experiments, particularly 2D correlations, can still be very time-consuming. In this regard, DNP has received considerable interest as a means to significantly increase the sensitivity of interphase layers. Jin et al. performed DNP-enhanced NMR experiments on a silicon nanowire anode to monitor the fate of 13C-labelled fluoroethylene carbonate electrolyte additive during cycling [37]. The DNP enhancement permitted a 13C double-quantum dipolar correlation spectrum to be recorded in only 9 hours, revealing through-space proximities that enabled the identification of at least six distinct molecular fragments in the SEI. it was also possible to probe the interfacial region between the SEI and the silicon substrate via a 2D heteronuclear correlation experiment recorded in just over 1 hour. The use of 1H–29Si CP in this experiment ensured that it was selective for only Si species within a few Ångstroms from 1H (i.e., those at the interface) and the observed resonances were attributed to a range of alkoxy- and hydroxylated Si environments. Exogenous DNP NMR can also be used in tandem with 13C enrichment to gain spatial selectivity over the outer surface and buried interfaces. This approach was used by Leskes et al. to study SEIs formed on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) anodes. In this work, it was found that DNP preferentially enhances the outer layers of the SEI due to the inaccessibility of the rGO pores to the bulky TEKPol radical used in the polarising agent. The results suggested that the outer SEI (enhanced via DNP) contains a higher number of aliphatic environments compared to the inner regions enhanced using enrichment, due to there being more peaks in the region 0 – 50 ppm. On the other hand, 13C enrichment was sufficient to give good sensitivity for the greater proportion of the SEI contained within the pores. These regions were found to contain more carboxylate (formate/oxalate) environments, based on the higher intensity of characteristic carboxylate resonances [32].

Hestenes et al. have used DNP-enhanced 13C solid-state NMR in conjunction with 1H and 13C solution-state NMR to identify chemical constituents in the CEI formed on a Li2RuO3 cathode [34]. The species identified include PEO fragments, lithium carbonates, and lithium acetates, based on the resonances present at δ = 68, 160, and 178 ppm, respectively, suggesting that the overall chemical composition is similar to SEIs observed on anode materials. Spectra were compared for samples at different states of charge, and it was found that resonances corresponding to PEO and Li salts were much lower intensity for samples in the charged state. The absence of these CEI components on charged cathodes indicates that they redissolve and migrate to the anode during charge, supporting the idea of anode–cathode “crosstalk” during cycling.

For metallic anodes, such as Li, electrons within the metal can also be used as the polarisation source for DNP NMR. This allows for the study of the interface between the SEI and the anode, a region not typically accessible via other surface characterisation techniques, such as by XPS. Hope et al. used this method to acquire 1H DNP-enhanced NMR spectra in order to study the impact of a fluoroethylene carbonate electrolyte additive, which reduces PEO formation whilst increasing poly(vinylene carbonate) formation [36]. What is particularly interesting about this study is that it was performed at room temperature as opposed to around 100 K, which is normally used for DNP experiments. This opens up the possibility for DNP-enhanced in situ NMR experiments which need to be performed at ambient temperature to allow the in situ battery cell to undergo electrochemical cycling. Furthermore, using the anode itself as the polarisation source avoids any potential surface modifications or chemical interference with the sample that could be caused by adding a radical solution. It is important to consider, however, that whilst the enhancements afforded are practically significant (up to a factor of 12), the enhancements achieved in this study were significantly smaller than those typically obtained using exogenous polarising agents at lower temperatures.

A wide range of inorganic oxides have been investigated for use as coatings in batteries, with the overall aim of increasing capacity retention of cathodes during extended cycling. Whilst a number of different oxides have been successfully implemented, Al2O3 coatings have been recognised as being versatile and easy to produce [38]. These typically consist of a thin layer of amorphous Al2O3 (several nm thick) coated directly onto the active cathode material (Figure 1). Optimised coatings of 0.2 wt% are estimated to cover ∼13% of the cathode particles [39]. The coatings can be prepared via wet chemical methods, which often involves the precipitation of the coating or coating precursors by solution-based methods followed by an annealing step, or atomic layer deposition (ALD). The coated cathode particles are then combined with binders and conductive carbon additives and processed into an electrode [17]. The thin and disordered nature of these coatings means that they are difficult to characterise and are not generally observable, e.g., by X-ray diffraction. An overarching question has been to understand the structural properties of the Al2O3, in particular at the interface between the coating and the cathode particle surface, since this is critical to ion transport into and out of the cathode. As discussed above, solid-state NMR can provide further information about the local structure of coatings, both in the bulk and at the surfaces/interfaces. In particular, 27Al MAS NMR can provide valuable information about the local structure of aluminium via the well-known dependence of the chemical shift on the local coordination environment. Four-, five- and six-coordinate Al–O species are associated with chemical shift ranges of 50 – 80 ppm, 30 – 40 ppm, and 10 – 15 ppm, respectively [40].

Initial NMR studies focused on Al2O3 coatings on LCO. This substrate has the advantage that it is diamagnetic in the fully lithiated state, so 27Al chemical shifts are not perturbed by paramagnetic interactions. These studies revealed that the synthetic method has a significant influence on the local structure and composition within the resulting coating. Coatings synthesised by wet chemical methods, as those used by Lee et al. and Fey et al., were found to be made up of four- and six-coordinate Al environments [41,42], whereas a pH-controlled precipitation method used by Kosova et al. resulted in an aluminium oxyhydroxide coating containing six-coordinate Al environments [43]. For both types of coating, low-intensity resonance patterns characteristic of Al in structurally ordered but compositionally disordered environments were also observed [43,44]. These patterns showed close resemblance to LiCo1−xAlxO2, which has a distinctive NMR spectrum consisting of a series of resonances between δ = 20 and 62 ppm - corresponding to Al environments with a variety of next-nearest neighbours (ranging from 0 to 6 Co) [45]. This constitutes strong evidence that mixing occurs across the coating-cathode interface during synthesis to form an outer layer of LiCo1−xAlxO2 around the bulk cathode particles. Furthermore, it is noted that at higher annealing temperature, a fully Al-doped system is formed (Figure 4 ) [43,44].

Since the initial studies, 27Al MAS NMR has also been expanded to include coatings on other cathodes. Pol et al. studied alumina coatings on Li1.2Ni0.175Mn0.525Co0.1O2 which had been made via a pulsed-sonication method [46]. The NMR data suggest the resulting coating contains primarily six-coordinate Al species, and after heat treatment an additional broad resonance (δ = 25–100 ppm) was observed that was suggested to be due to LiCo1−xAlxO2 or LiAlO2. Furthermore, a wet chemical method used by Negi et al. to coat NMC (LiCo1 − x − yMnxNiyO2) resulted in the presence of four-, five-, and six-coordinate Al species as observed by 27Al NMR. Here, the presence of LiAlO2 was also confirmed by XPS [47]. Four-coordinate γ-LiAlO2 (at δ ≈ 69 ppm) was also observed via NMR by Han et al. for coatings on NMC532 (LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2) [44,48]. These studies were expanded to a variety of NMC-based systems, and it was shown that changing the composition to NMC662 (LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2) and NMC811 (LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2) allowed for the diffusion of Al into the bulk of the NMC system, in addition to the formation of a LiAlO2/Al2O3 coating [49]. This suggests that the exact composition of the substrate directly influences the processes occurring during coating formation. Thin coatings of Al2O3 (0.5 wt%) on NMC532 have also been studied via 27Al MAS NMR and compared to coatings formed by ALD. Here, the NMR data suggests that the high temperature annealing step had a greater effect on the structure and morphology of the coatings prepared by wet chemical methods than by ALD. These studies also identified the presence of both six-coordinate α-LiAlO2 (δ ≈ 14 ppm) and four-coordinate γ-LiAlO2. It was noted by the authors that as the coating thickness increased from 0.5 wt% to 2 wt% the dominant phase changed from α-LiAlO2 to γ-LiAlO2. The proportion of γ-LiAlO2 then increased further has the thickness was increased to 5 wt%. This was rationalised by the better lattice matching between NMC and α-LiAlO2 [17].

In addition to layered oxides, spinel cathodes have also been coated in protective layers of alumina. Mereacre et al. studied Al2O3 coatings on the high-voltage spinel LiNi0.5Mn1.5O2 (LNMO). Here, 27Al MAS NMR studies were used, in conjunction with other complementary techniques, to study the structure of the coating. The 27Al MAS NMR data suggests the presence of six-coordinate Al environments within the coating, similar to those seen for coatings on LCO and NMC. The absence of additional resonances in the 27Al and 7Li MAS NMR spectra (Figure 5 (a) and (b)) suggest that there is little/no mixing at the coating-cathode interface (as seen with LCO and NMC) [26].

Although 27Al MAS NMR data can provide valuable insight into the structure of the coating, 27Al is not the only nucleus that can be used to study these systems. Due to the presence of lithium-containing phases at the coating-cathode interface, 6Li and 7Li can also be used to probe this interface [26,17,41,48,48,49]. However, this does require that lithium within the cathode can be differentiated from lithium within the interface. Typically, cathode materials such as NMC and LNMO are paramagnetic and, as a result, their NMR spectra are influenced by the paramagnetic interaction. For example, the Li environment within LNMO has been reported to exhibit a chemical shift of δ ≈ 890 ppm, which is far outside the typical diamagnetic 7Li chemical shift range of ±2 ppm. This can allow the presence of diamagnetic LiAlO2 to be confirmed or ruled out [26]. It is worth noting that due to the small chemical shift range for 6,7Li makes it less straightforward to distinguish between different diamagnetic phases, meaning that the signals observed near δ = 0 ppm can also contain contributions from common diamagnetic impurities, e.g., Li2CO3, which is commonly formed as a low-level impurity during synthesis [49].

Polymeric materials have advantages as coatings for electrode materials due to their mechanical greater flexibility. This can allow them to better accommodate volumetric changes during cycling without cracking, which would lead to further interphase formation when fresh electrode surface is exposed to the electrolyte. This is potentially very useful for sulfur cathodes, which undergo a large volume expansion during cycling (around 180%). Furthermore, conducting polymers, such as PEDOT can be used to increase electronic conductivity between sulfur particles, from around 10−30 S cm−1 to up to 10−5 S cm−1. Shafique et al. used 13C CPMAS NMR spectra to verify the presence of the PEDOT coating created on a sulfur cathode by dielectric barrier discharge, a technique that creates coatings from plasmas. Identification of PEDOT was based on the chemical shift values of the aromatic region (110 < δ < 160 ppm) and CH2–O peak (δ ≈ 66 ppm) that had previously been reported. In addition to these peaks, they also observed an aliphatic peak at 30 ppm. This was interpreted as CH3 groups formed by CH2–O bond cleavage, as a result of the plasma treatment [15].

An alternative approach to using solid-state NMR to directly probe the coating structure is to instead use it to study the effect of the coating on the electrode properties and behaviour during cycling. For example, Lennartz et al. used 7Li operando NMR to compare dendrite growth on coated and uncoated lithium metal anodes during cycling. Dendrites are clearly distinguished by a significant difference in 7Li chemical shift as compared to the bulk metal (δ ≈ 267 and 245 ppm, respectively). By monitoring the intensities of the dendrite and bulk metal resonances over multiple Li stripping and plating cycles, it was found that a polyborosiloxane coating effectively suppressed dendrite growth as compared to an uncoated lithium anode [50].

Alkylated lithium silicates have beneficial properties as cathode coatings due to their low reactivity towards oxygen species, their open structures which facilitate good ion transport, and their affinity for scavenging contaminants such as HF. However, these coatings are extremely thin (2–5 nm), making them challenging to characterise [51]. However, DNP NMR can be used to study how the chemistry of the coating changes throughout its thickness. As mentioned, DNP preferentially enhances the region of the sample closest to the electron source. This typically means surface enhancement, through external radicals, but if the substrate is doped with paramagnetic species, then the inner layer of the coating can be studied as well. This is the approach adopted by Haber et al., who used Fe(III)-doped TiO2 as a substrate. This revealed that the coating structure is not uniform in composition. The surface is dominated by alkylated environments such as R3–SiOR’, whereas the region closest to the substrate is made up of lesser alkylated environments like Si(OSi)4 and RSi(OSi)3 (Figure 6 ) [31].

As with all coatings, it is important to understand the impact they have on ion mobility within the cell, compared to a naturally occurring CEI. This can be studied to an extent via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, but it can be challenging to assign the features of the Nyquist plot or components in the impedance spectra for a cell containing two electrodes. Solid-state NMR offers an alternative to this, as both 6Li and 7Li can be observed, but have different natural abundances (7.6% and 92.4% respectively). Haber et al. exploited this by pre-cycling their cathodes against a 6Li-enriched anode, which artificially increased the amount of 6Li in the cathode. The cathodes were then immersed in an LP30 electrolyte solution (1 M LiPF6 in a 1:1 EC/DMC mixture) at natural abundance. The 6Li concentration was then tracked as a function of time as the 6Li+ ions diffused into the electrolyte and exchanged with 7Li+ ions (Figure 6 (e)). The coated cathode showed superior lithium-ion mobility, with almost 50% of the lithium being exchanged after 80 hours, compared to just 10% in the uncoated cathode with a naturally formed CEI [31].

Solid-state NMR spectroscopy is emerging as a valuable technique for studying coatings and interfaces within batteries due to its ability to provide structural and mechanistic insight in disordered and complex systems that are challenging study by other methods. The element specificity of NMR can provide clarity for multicomponent and disordered systems, which is advantageous when studying the complex compositions of interphases and coatings. Although the extremely thin nature of these coatings and interfaces can pose sensitivity challenges, recent experimental developments, such as DNP, have provided the additional sensitivity required to perform experiments that would otherwise not be feasible. Furthermore, DNP NMR experiments can be selective for specific regions of the sample, e.g., the outer SEI by exogenous DNP or the coating-electrode interface by endogenous DNP.

As new battery chemistries are developed and current systems are further optimised, solid-state NMR will continue to be a powerful tool for understanding the structure and mechanisms occurring at interfaces. Recent advances in ultra-fast MAS NMR (νR > 110 kHz) show considerable benefits for the study of paramagnetic systems, particularly when combined with lower magnetic field spectrometers [52,53]. While this offers promise for the study of coatings on paramagnetic materials, such as some cathodes, the necessarily small samples volumes within small diameter MAS rotors will exacerbate the sensitivity issues already observed for the study of thin coatings and interfaces. DNP signal enhancement can potentially mitigate this issue; however, DNP enhancements typically decrease with increasing MAS rate. New polarising agents show increasing promise for DNP NMR under fast MAS, but these will require further study and optimisation before they become widely available [54,55].

An ongoing consideration for the study of battery interfaces by NMR is that they can be metastable or reactive and, in some cases, dissembling the battery for ex situ measurements may cause structural changes. In addition, the introduction of exogenous polarising agents for DNP carries with it the risk of further chemical interference with the system. In this regard, we envisage that endogenous DNP will become an increasingly powerful approach for the study of battery materials and interfaces, although this technique remains in its infancy and requires further exploration and optimisation. In situ experiments can also potentially provide useful information about intermediate species forming within the cell during cycling. However, for some systems the detail that can be obtained is limited owing to the fact that most in situ solid-state NMR protocols only allow for the acquisition of static data. A pseudo in situ protocol for MAS NMR studies of batteries has recently been demonstrated by Goward and coworkers [56,57]. We anticipate that this approach will be very powerful for the study of full cell batteries; however, it remains to be determined if the additional resolution afforded can allow the low intensity and often broad signals associated with interfaces to be observed in the presence of signals attributed to the rest of the cell, e.g., electrodes and electrolyte.
